title: The Fitzpatrick Moment: Alex's Maiden DP World Tour Title
excerpt: England's Alex Fitzpatrick clinched his maiden DP World Tour title at the Indian Open, with brother Matt winning on the PGA Tour last week — a rare sibling achievement.
body:
A Week for the Fitzpatricks

England's Alex Fitzpatrick wrote his name into DP World Tour history on Sunday, capturing his maiden title at the 2026 Hero Indian Open in Gurugram with a two-stroke victory over defending champion Eugenio Chacarra. The 27-year-old's triumph is made all the sweeter by timing: his brother Matt claimed the Valspar Championship on the PGA Tour just eight days earlier, vaulting to a career-high world ranking of No. 6. It's the kind of week golf families dream of.

Alex's Winning Score
-9
Margin of Victory
2 strokes
Matt's World Ranking
No. 6
Combined Wins This Week
2

The Comeback Round

Fitzpatrick entered Sunday trailing Chacarra by four strokes, but a composed 3-under final round propelled him past the defending champion, who stumbled with a 3-over closing card. After carding a double bogey on the 18th — an uncharacteristic finish — Alex still had enough cushion to secure the trophy. The DLF Golf and Country Club's punishing greens and demanding setup brought out precision golf from Fitzpatrick when it mattered most.

The Brotherhood Bond

"I idolise him, so just trying to be like him in every way," Alex said of Matt after his triumph. The siblings are preparing to reunite in Florida later this week, where the family will gather to celebrate what has been an extraordinary stretch for the Fitzpatricks. Matt's ascent to world No. 6 represents his highest ranking ever, built on a foundation of consistent European Tour success and a breakthrough major championship at the U.S. Open. Alex's maiden title — hard-won over 72 holes in India — represents his first step toward matching his brother's trajectory.

The Indian Open Narrative

For a tournament steeped in history on the Asian Swing, the 2026 Indian Open took on special significance as Alex claimed his breakthrough victory on Indian soil. Among local contenders, teenager Manoj S and Om Prakash Chouhan recovered from difficult weeks to post a tie for 43rd, whilst Olympian Shubhankar Sharma, a two-time Asian Tour winner, failed to make the cut. South Africa's MJ Daffue tied for third, whilst Francesco Molinari rounded out the elite finishers in sixth. But this was Fitzpatrick's day — and by extension, a banner week for English golf.

Eyes on Augusta

With the Masters just nine days away, both Fitzpatricks now head to Augusta with momentum and confidence coursing through their veins. Matt, already established as a major championship winner with his U.S. Open pedigree, arrives as a top-10 threat. Alex, riding the emotional high of his maiden tour title, will be hunting for an invitation or OWGR qualifying position. For now, though, the focus is on family, celebration, and the validation of years of hard work — for both brothers.
